plainMeaning: Here the worshipper bows to eight of the twenty-four Jinas who are still to be born in the coming age — Padmanābha, Suradeva, Supārśva, Svayamprabha, Sarvānubhūti, Devasuta, Udaya and Peḍhāla.
recitation: (ḍhāḷa trījī) padmanābha suradeva, supārśva svayamprabhu hoi. sarvānubhūti devasuta, udaya peḍhālaja joi.

wordByWord.gloss: (third dhāḷa) — a rubric marking the start of the third melodic section of the hymn
wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati ḍhāḷa 'tune, melodic unit' + trījī 'third' (< Skt. tṛtīyā)
wordByWord.notes: A performance heading, not a metrical word of the couplet; this dhāḷa turns from the present age to the anāgata (future) caubīsī.

wordByWord.gloss: Padmanābha ('lotus-naveled one') — the first Jina of the coming age
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. padma 'lotus' + nābhi 'navel'; tatpuruṣa/bahuvrīhi 'having a lotus at the navel'
wordByWord.notes: By tradition the soul of King Śreṇika (Bimbisāra) will arise as this first future Tīrthaṅkara.

wordByWord.gloss: Suradeva ('lord of the gods') — the second future Jina
Supārśva ('the fair-sided one') — the third future Jina
wordByWord.notes: Distinct from Supārśvanātha, the 7th Jina of the present age, despite the shared name.

wordByWord.gloss: Svayamprabha ('the self-luminous lord') — the fourth future Jina
wordByWord.notes: Book form svayamprabhu for metre; the standard name is Svayamprabha.

wordByWord.gloss: are / shall be — a connecting devotional verb closing the first line
wordByWord.etymology: Old Gujarati / Apabhraṃśa hoï 'becomes, is' (< Prakrit hoi < Skt. bhavati)
wordByWord.notes: Rhymes with joi at the close of line two; functions as a metrical-devotional copula, 'they are (honoured)'.

wordByWord.gloss: Sarvānubhūti ('the all-perceiving one') — the fifth future Jina
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. sarva 'all' + anubhūti 'experience, perception'; sandhi sarva + anubhūti → sarvānubhūti
wordByWord.notes: Lexicons (Monier-Williams) gloss the name 'all-perceiving' and record it as the name of a Jina of a future era.

wordByWord.gloss: Devasuta ('son of the gods') — the sixth future Jina
wordByWord.notes: The book's reading; in the wider Śvetāmbara list this Jina is usually named Devaśruta/Devaśruti, of which Devasut is a vernacular variant.

wordByWord.gloss: Udaya ('the rising, the dawn') — the seventh future Jina
Peḍhāla(putra) ('the one born of Peḍhāla') — the eighth future Jina
wordByWord.etymology: Peḍhāla (proper name) + -ja 'born of' (< Skt. -ja); equivalent to Peḍhālaputra
wordByWord.notes: Vernacular Peḍhālaja answers to the standard list-name Pedhālaputra/Peḍhāla.

wordByWord.gloss: behold / contemplate — closing devotional verb of the couplet
wordByWord.etymology: Old Gujarati joï 'to see, behold' (< Prakrit joï < Skt. √dyut/dṛś, cf. dyotate 'shines forth')
wordByWord.notes: Paired with hoi as an end-rhyme; invites the reciter to 'look upon' these coming Jinas in the mind's eye.

literalTranslation: (Third dhāḷa.) Padmanābha, Suradeva, Supārśva, Svayamprabha there are; Sarvānubhūti, Devasuta, Udaya — behold Peḍhāla(putra) too.
idiomaticTranslation: Now bow to the Jinas yet to come: Padmanābha, Suradeva, Supārśva and self-radiant Svayamprabha — these they are; and Sarvānubhūti, Devasuta and Udaya, and Peḍhāla too — behold them all in reverence.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: With the rubric (ḍhāḷa-trījī) the hymn pivots from the Jinas who have already walked the earth to those not yet born, and this opening couplet names the first eight of that coming twenty-four: Padmanābha, Suradeva, Supārśva, Svayamprabha, Sarvānubhūti, Devasuta, Udaya and Peḍhāla. The craftsmanship is simple and deliberate — a bare litany of names framed by two rhyming vernacular verbs, hoi ('they are') and joi ('behold'). That pairing is the whole devotional engine of the verse: the worshipper does not narrate these Jinas' deeds (they have none yet) but simply affirms that they 'are' worthy of homage and 'looks upon' them across the gulf of time. The list flows straight on from the close of the present caubīsī in the preceding lines (Pārśva, Vīra, 'the present Jinas') and continues unbroken into the next verse (Potila, Śatakīrti, Munisuvrata, Amama …), so this couplet is one breath of an unhurried roll-call. Several names deliberately echo present-age Jinas — Supārśva, Munisuvrata further on — underscoring that the wheel of the Tīrtha turns the same shapes again in every age. Note too the gentle internal meaning of the names themselves: 'lotus-naveled,' 'self-luminous,' 'all-perceiving,' 'the dawn' — epithets of awakening that already shimmer before these souls have taken birth.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse rests on the Jain teaching of the anāgata caubīsī — the twenty-four Tīrthaṅkaras who will arise in the coming ascending half-cycle (utsarpiṇī). A soul becomes a Tīrthaṅkara through tīrthaṅkara-nāma-karma already bound in a prior life, so these future Jinas are not abstractions but identifiable beings even now travelling toward Jinahood; the most famous instance, carried by the very first name here, is the tradition that King Śreṇika (Bimbisāra) — Mahāvīra's royal devotee — will be reborn as Padmanābha, the first Jina of the next age. To venerate them is therefore to bow to Jinahood as a timeless reality, equally real in past, present and future, and to affirm that the Tīrtha never finally fails: awakened guides will come again. This is the same impulse that the hymn elsewhere expresses by sweeping across space; here it sweeps across time.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Reciting these names trains the heart to revere awakening even where it has not yet appeared — to honour potential, not only accomplishment. Pausing on hoi and joi, the practitioner can let 'they are' steady faith that the path is perennial and let 'behold' become an actual inward glance at these coming Jinas, dissolving anxiety about living in a spiritually 'late' age. It is also a quiet teaching in hope: the soul itself, rightly cultivated, stands within this same lineage of possibility.

crossReferences.text: Lists of the twenty-four anāgata Tīrthaṅkaras (Padmanābha … beginning with the rebirth of Śreṇika)
Sarvānubhūti named as a Jina of a future era ('all-perceiving')
crossReferences.source: Monier-Williams, A Sanskrit-English Dictionary, s.v. sarvānubhūti

sources.title: Sarvānubhūti — Sanskrit dictionary definitions (Monier-Williams; Shabda-Sagara)
sources.consultedFor: Confirming Sarvānubhūti as the name of a future-era Jina and its sense 'all-perceiving' (sarva + anubhūti).

sources.title: Padmanābha — definitions (Cologne lexica via Wisdom Library)
sources.consultedFor: Etymology of Padmanābha ('lotus-naveled') and its use as a proper name.

sources.title: Supārśva — Sanskrit/Jaina definitions
sources.consultedFor: Sense of Supārśva ('fair-sided', su + pārśva) and its occurrence as a Jina-name.

sources.title: Tirthankara
sources.consultedFor: Framing of tīrthaṅkara-nāma-karma and the doctrine that twenty-four Jinas arise in each ascending/descending age-cycle, grounding the anāgata caubīsī.

notes: The couplet is a plain roll-call, so the commentary is kept tight. Verbatim book readings are preserved: svayamprabhu (for standard Svayamprabha), devasut (the wider list's Devaśruta/Devaśruti), and peḍhālaja (= Pedhālaputra/Peḍhāla). hoi and joi are Old-Gujarati/Apabhraṃśa rhyming verbs ('are' / 'behold') used as metrical-devotional fillers rather than narrative predicates. The identification of Padmanābha with the future birth of King Śreṇika (Bimbisāra) is well-attested Śvetāmbara tradition but is a doctrinal-traditional claim, not a lexical one. Spellings of these future-Jina names vary across editions and online lists; the names here follow the book's verse plus its surrounding verses (which continue Potila, Śatakīrti, Munisuvrata, etc.), treated as the golden source over the inconsistent secondary lists encountered in general search.
